Reasoning: The Rh system consists of 60 antigens (antigens A, B, C, D, E and F). These antigens are present on RBCs and their corresponding antibodies are produced against them.
The most impoant antigen is D which is responsible for erythroblastosis fetalis.
Anti-D antibody can be formed either due to sensitization during pregnancy or transfusion with blood containing anti-D antibodies.
In case of sensitization during pregnancy, it occurs when fetal red cells cross placenta into maternal circulation. This usually happens at term because of rupture of membranes.
If mother has already been sensitized before pregnancy, she will have anti-D antibodies circulating in her body. During subsequent pregnancies, these antibodies may attack the fetal red cells crossing the placenta leading to hemolytic disease of newborn.
This condition is called erythroblastosis fetalis.
Reference : page no;123 Ghai essential pediatrics eighth edition

Since there are 1000 mL of blood in one liter, and 1000 liters in one gallon, the total volume of blood in this individual is approximately 96.6 L. Reasoning: The concentration is calculated using the formula C= D x V / F where C is concentration, D is dose, V is volume, and F is fluid volume. In this case, we want to find out how many ml of the drug will be needed for each drop given. So, we can rearrange the equation to solve for the concentration: C = D / (V/F). This means that if we give one drop per minute, the concentration would be equal to the dose divided by the volume over time. If we use the same logic but instead of finding the concentration, we can calculate the number of drops required to deliver a certain amount of drug. We know that the total volume of the solution is 250 mL, so we need to divide the total volume by the desired flow rate to get the number of drops per minute. Thus, the number of drops per minute is 250 mL / 1 mL/drop = 250 drops/minute. Now, we can determine the concentration of the drug in the solution by dividing the dose by the volume over time. For example, if we administer 100 mg of the drug in 250 mL of solution over 3 minutes, the concentration would be 100 mg / (250 mL / 3 min) = 12.5 mg/mL. However, since we do not know the exact volume of blood passing through the veins every minute, we cannot accurately calculate the concentration of the drug in the bloodstream. Instead, we must assume that the concentration remains constant throughout the infusion period. Therefore, the concentration of the drug in the bloodstream after 3 minutes would still be 12.5 mg/mL. Since the concentration of the drug in the bloodstream is 12.5 mg/mL, we can now calculate the dosage of the drug administered during the first hour. To do this, we multiply the concentration by the duration of the infusion: 12.5 mg/mL * 3600 seconds = 45000 mg = 45 g. Ref: Katzung 11th ed Pg 79

Reasoning: The lingual nerve (CN V3) supplies the posterior 1/3rd of the tongue and carries taste sensations from this area. It also provides motor supply to intrinsic muscles of the tongue. Damage to the lingual nerve results in loss of taste on the posterior third of the tongue. The chorda tympani branch of CN VII innervates the geniculate ganglion which gives rise to the glossopharyngeal nerve. This nerve has no direct connection with the facial or pharyngeal branches of the vagus nerve. Therefore damage to the chorda tympani will not cause deviation of the tongue to either side. The trigeminal nerve does not have any connections with the lingual nerve. Hence injury to the trigeminal nerve would not affect the innervation of the tongue.

We will have some error because it is unlikely that our sample represents exactly the same distribution of blood pressures found among all adults in the country. This error is called sampling error.* To reduce the chance of making errors, we use a confidence interval. In general, a 95% confidence interval means that there is only one in 20 chances that the true mean blood pressure falls outside the interval.* If we were to repeat the experiment many times, each time taking a new random sample, about 95 out of 100 intervals would contain the true mean blood pressure.* The width of the interval depends on the standard deviation of the data. Larger the SD, wider the interval; smaller the SD, narrower the interval.* Sample Size CalculationFormula: n = Z^2 * p(1 - p)/d^2Where:n = sample sizep = estimated proportionq = desired margin of error (confidence limit)Z = z score corresponding to the desired confidence level (e.g., 1.96 for 95% confidence)d = precision or standard error of the estimate

Reasoning: Cystic Fibrosis 


It is an autosomal recessive disease caused due to mutation in gene encoding for CFTR protein.
The most common mutations are deletion or substitution of phenylalanine at position 508 and G551del.
Defective chloride channel function results in abnormal secretion of thick mucus into respiratory tract and pancreatic duct system.
Abnormalities in ion channels cause defect in sodium absorption from intestine leading to malabsorption of nutrients.
In lungs there is chronic infection with pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus.
There is increased risk of bronchiectasis, pneumonia, emphysema, pulmonary fibrosis and lung cancer.
Pancreatic insufficiency causes steatorrhea, malnutrition and diabetes mellitus.
Other features include meconium ileus, hepatosplenomegaly, clubbing, retinitis pigmentosa, scoliosis, infertility, azoospermia, hypogonadism, osteopenia, osteoporosis, chondrocalcinosis, corneal dystrophy, ichthyosis, alopecia, sinusitis, otitis media, hearing loss, mental retardation, cirrhosis, gallstones, pancreatitis, intestinal pseudo polyps, colonic polyposis, rectal prolapse, and hemoptysis.

Epiglottis is an oval cartilaginous plate that lies between the arytenoid cailage and thyroid cailage. It has two folds - anterior (vocal fold) and posterior (epiglottal fold). The vocal fold is attached to the arytenoid cailages while the epiglottal fold is attached to the base of the tongue. The epiglottis is covered by mucous membrane except at its tip where it is bare. The mucosa is continuous with the pharynx. The larynx is divided into three pas : - Cuneiform pa (cuneus): This part extends from the angle formed by the junction of the thyroid and cricoid cailages to the upper border of the epiglottis. It contains the corniculate and pyramidal cailages. Corniculate cailage is present only on one side and pyramidal cailage is absent in males but present in females. Pyramidal cailage is also absent in infants. Cricoid pa: This pa extends from the lower border of the epiglottis to the level of the vocal cords. It contains the cricoid cailage. Arytenoid pa: This pa extends from the vocal cord to the root of the tongue. It contains the arytenoid cailages. Ref: PL Dhingra Textbook of Ear, Nose & Throat; Edition 7th; Page no: 102

Reasoning: Ans. is 'a' i.e., Champy's plate; 'b' i.e., Lag screw; 'c' i.e., Reconstruction plate Treatment for mandibular fractureo Mandibular fractures can be treated with or without internal fixation depending on the type and severity of the injury. The most common types of mandibular fractures include:1. Closed mandibular fracture (no displacement): It may require no treatment if it does not cause pain or difficulty swallowing.2. Open mandibular fracture (displaced fracture): This requires surgical intervention to restore normal alignment of the teeth and bone fragments.3. Compound mandibular fracture (open fracture): This involves an open wound that exposes the underlying bone and soft tissues.4. Fractures involving the condylar process: These fractures often involve both the body and neck of the mandible.5. Fractures involving the angle of the mandible: These fractures usually occur at the junction between the body and ramus of the mandible.6. Fractures involving the mentalis nerve: These fractures typically affect the inferior alveolar branch of the trigeminal nerve.7. Fractures involving the temporomandibular joint (TMJ): These injuries can range from minor sprains to severe dislocations.Treatment options for mandibular fractures include:Nonoperative management: Nonoperative management includes observation, analgesics, antibiotics, and oral hygiene measures. Operative management: Surgical management depends on the location and nature of the fracture. A variety of techniques have been described for treating mandibular fractures, including:Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF): ORIF is the most commonly used method for stabilizing displaced mandibular fractures. In this procedure, the fractured ends of the mandible are brought into proper alignment using plates, screws, wires, or pins.Closed reduction and external immobilization: This technique uses a splint to hold the broken pieces together until they heal. It is useful when there is significant swelling or bleeding around the fracture site.Reconstruction plate: This is a metal plate placed over the fracture site to stabilize the bones while they heal. It is used when the fracture cannot be reduced surgically because of swelling or other complications.Champy's plate: This is a special type of reconstruction plate designed specifically for mandibular fractures. It has holes along its length so that it can be cut down to fit the specific shape of the jawbone.Lag screw: This is a small screw inserted through the mandible to help keep the two halves of the bone aligned during healing. It is used in conjunction with a reconstruction plate.

Reasoning: Peripheral arterial disease (PAD), also known as peripheral vascular disease or arteriosclerosis, is an impoant cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide. It is characterized by atherosclerosis of the arteries supplying the limbs. PAD can be classified into two types: intermittent claudication and ischemic ulcers/gangrene. Intermittent claudication occurs when walking causes pain due to insufficient blood flow to the muscles of the legs. Ischemic ulcers occur when there is complete blockage of the artery. A high risk of amputation exists if gangrene develops. Risk factors include smoking, diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, age, male sex, family history, and obesity. The ankle-brachial index (ABI) is calculated by dividing systolic BP in the foot by systolic BP in the arm. An ABI less than 0.9 suggests peripheral vascular disease. Exercise increases the ABI because it improves circulation through increased cardiac output and decreased resistance. L-arginine is a precursor of nitric oxide, which relaxes smooth muscle cells and provides endothelium-independent vasodilation. Smoking is associated with a higher incidence of peripheral vascular disease than coronary vascular disease. (b) Due to adherence to the capillary endothelium, they are not seen in peripheral blood smearRef: Ananthanarayan 9th ed./p.128* The parasites that cause malaria are obligate intracellular protozoa.* In humans, the life cycle is completed with two hosts - female anopheles mosquitoes and man.* Malaria parasite has four morphological forms: gametocyte, zygote, merozoites and schizogony stage. Gametocytes are the sexual form which remain dormant in human host until ingested by another mosquito. Zygotes develop into sporozoites within the midgut of the mosquito. Sporozoites migrate through the salivary glands of the mosquito and infect the next host when the mosquito takes a blood meal from the infected person. After entering the red cells of the new host, the sporozoites transform into merozoites and invade other RBCs. Merozoites undergo asexual reproduction to produce more merozoites. These merozoites eventually mature into ring forms, trophozoites and finally schizozoites. When the schistozoites reach maturity, they rupture out of the RBCs and enter the bloodstream where they become gametocytes.

It is given by the rule of nines. This method is most accurate when the patient has normal proportions.The rule of nines is applied to estimate the percentage of body surface area that is burned. These regions include the head, neck, anterior chest, posterior chest, arms, legs, groin, perineum, buttocks, and back. The sum of these nine areas represents approximately 100% of the body surface area. Reasoning: Ans. D. FRCRecoil is defined as the ability of lungs to expand against an external pressure (or resistance). The elastic recoil capacity of the lungs is determined by the compliance of the lung parenchyma and the chest wall. When the lungs are fully expanded, they have reached their maximum possible size - this point is called the total lung capacity (TLC), which represents the sum of all volumes within the thoracic cavity including residual air. At rest, the lung has returned to its normal resting position after expiration; this process is known as relaxation or recoil. This occurs when the internal pressure of the lungs falls below atmospheric pressure. The amount of work required for this process is termed the respiratory system's energy cost.The force that opposes expansion during inspiration is referred to as the resistive load on the lungs. It includes the static compression of the chest wall and the dynamic compression of the airways due to contraction of the muscles involved in breathing. The reciprocal relationship between inspiratory effort and the resulting increase in tidal volume is described by the slope of the curve representing the relation between these two variables. A steeper slope indicates greater respiratory drive and more forceful inspiration.

Reasoning: Ans. is 'b' i.e., Stapedial otosclerosis o The audiogram shows normal air conduction (AC), and reduced bone conduction (BC).o This pattern suggests that there is conductive lesion at or near the ossicular chain.o In this case it is most likely to be stapes fixation due to otosclerosis.o In stapedial otosclerosis, the stapes footplate becomes fixed on its own axis so that it cannot move freely during vibration.o Thus, sound waves transmitted through the middle ear do not get amplified because they reach the oval window without being reflected back from the footplate.o So, the BC curve will show a flat line below the AC curve.o If the stapes footplate moves normally, the BC curve should lie above the AC curve.o In other words, if the stapes footplate does not fixate, the vibrations produced by the movement of the tympanic membrane will be transmitted down the ossicles and into the cochlea.o However, when the stapes footplate is fixed, the vibrations cannot pass through the footplate and thus no sound energy reaches the inner ear.o Therefore, the BC curve lies below the AC curve.o A positive Weber's test indicates lateralization of the auditory nerve. It means that stimulation of one ear produces more sensation in the opposite ear than in the stimulated ear.o For example, if you stimulate the right ear with an air wave, the person hears louder in their left ear than in their right ear.o A negative Weber's test indicates ipsilateralization of the auditory nerve. It means that stimulation of one ear produces more sensation in the same ear than in the opposite ear.o For example, if you stimulate the right ear with an air wave, the person hears louder in their right ear than in their left ear.o In stapedial otosclerosis, the vestibular function is usually preserved but may become impaired over time.o There is also evidence that patients with stapedial otosclerosis have increased risk for developing Meniere's disease.

Reasoning: Ans. is 'c' i.e., Vaccination coverage o The term ABC analysis (or A, B, C classification) is used to classify items into three categories based on their usage or consumption rate.o In health care setting, it is applied to drugs and supplies.Category A - High value/High frequency of use (20% of total stock). These should be kept in stock at all times. Category B - Medium value/Medium frequency of use (30%). These can be stocked only when there is sufficient space available. Category C - Low value/Low frequency of use (50%) - these may not need to be stocked at all.o This method helps in optimizing storage space and reducing wastage. It also helps in planning procurement.o For example, if 100 units of an antibiotic are stored, 60 will belong to category A, 30 to category B and 10 to category C.o If 40 units of vaccine are stored, 28 will belong to category A, 12 to category B and 8 to category C.o To implement this system effectively, one needs to know the annual demand of each item. So, we have to do ABC analysis every year.o However, since the data is collected annually, it takes time to analyze the data and make decisions about stocking levels. Therefore, some modifications have been suggested. One such modification is that instead of doing ABC analysis once a year, it is done quarterly. Another suggestion is to do ABC analysis monthly. But even with monthly analysis, the process becomes cumbersome because of large number of items.o Instead of using ABC analysis, another approach has been proposed which involves classifying the items according to their shelf life. Items with short shelf lives are placed in category A, medium shelf lives in category B and long shelf lives in category C.

Reasoning: The inflammatory response to injury or infection can be divided into four zones: (1) the area of injury itself; (2) the immediate surrounding tissue; (3) the vascular system that supplies blood to the injured site; and (4) the distant sites where mediators of inflammation may act on target cells. In the first three zones, there is an influx of leukocytes from the circulation into the tissues. This process is called diapedesis. Leukocytes migrate through the walls of capillaries and venules and enter the interstitial spaces. The leukocyte population includes neutrophils, monocytes, lymphocytes, eosinophils, and basophils. Neutrophils are most abundant among these cell types at the time of acute inflammation. Mononuclear phagocytic cells include monocytes and macrophages. Macrophages are derived from monocytes but have greater capacity for proliferation and differentiation than do monocytes. They also secrete more cytokines and chemokines than do monocytes.
